learn.title: The Duck Curve: Solar's Grid Challenge at Sunset
learn.intro: Solar power creates a unique grid challenge called the 'duck curve.' As solar generation peaks mid-day, grid operators must rapidly ramp up other sources at sunset when demand rises but solar drops. Understanding this helps design smarter grids with better storage and forecasting.
learn.c1_text: Like a duck's profile, the curve shows low morning demand, deep midday solar surplus, and steep evening ramp. California's grid sees solar supply drop 10 GW in just two hours after sunset, forcing gas plants to fire up quickly.
learn.c2_text: At 5-7 PM, electricity demand spikes while solar fades. Grid operators need fast-ramping resources to meet this 'ramp rate' of roughly 400 MW per minute. This stresses equipment and raises costs, requiring better battery storage and demand response systems.
learn.c3_text: India's 70 GW solar capacity faces similar duck curve risks. Western and southern grids with high solar penetration (like Rajasthan and Gujarat) experience steep evening ramps during peak months, requiring pumped hydro storage and flexible coal plants to balance supply.
learn.stat2: California: 10 GW solar drop in 2 hours at sunset
learn.stat3: India targets: 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030
learn.readLabel: Read at US Dept of Energy

learn.title: HVDC Transmission: Power Across Thousands of Kilometres
learn.intro: High-Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) lines transmit electricity over vast distances with minimal losses, connecting remote renewable sites to cities. This technology is essential for sharing solar and wind power across regions and countries efficiently.
learn.c1_text: Like a wide highway for electricity, HVDC loses only 3% per 1,000 km versus 7% for AC lines. It transmits power underwater and underground without capacitance limits, making it ideal for connecting offshore wind farms to shore or desert solar to cities.
learn.c2_text: China's 3,300 km Xiangjiaba-Shanghai link transmits 12 GW of hydropower at 800 kV. Europe plans North Sea offshore HVDC grids connecting wind farms across multiple countries, acting like an international power internet for renewable energy sharing.
learn.c3_text: India's 1,830 km Raigarh-Pugalur HVDC line transfers 6 GW from Chhattisgarh coal to southern states at ±800 kV. The planned Green Energy Corridor will use HVDC to move solar power from Rajasthan to load centres in Maharashtra and Gujarat with minimal losses.
learn.stat2: China: 30+ HVDC lines operating over 20,000 km
learn.stat3: Transmission loss reduction: HVDC saves 4% vs AC per 1,000 km

learn.title: Solid-State Batteries: Safer, Denser Energy Storage
learn.intro: Solid-state batteries replace liquid electrolytes with solid materials, making them safer and more energy-dense than lithium-ion. They promise longer EV range, faster charging, and reduced fire risk — key for scaling electric transport and grid storage.
learn.c1_text: Conventional lithium-ion uses flammable liquid electrolytes — like water in a battery. Solid-state uses ceramic or polymer solids, preventing leaks and thermal runaway. Energy density jumps from 250 Wh/kg to over 500 Wh/kg, doubling EV range potential.
learn.c2_text: Toyota plans solid-state batteries for 2027-28 EVs achieving 1,000 km range with 10-minute fast charging. The solid separator prevents dendrite growth that causes short circuits, reducing fire risk by 90% compared to liquid lithium-ion cells in current EVs.
learn.c3_text: India's Battery500 Consortium aims to develop solid-state batteries through IIT Madras and IISc partnerships. The National Battery Mission targets 50 GWh domestic manufacturing by 2030, with solid-state R&D funding of ₹1,800 crore to reduce import dependence on China.
learn.stat1: Energy density: Solid-state 500 Wh/kg vs Li-ion 250 Wh/kg
learn.stat2: Toyota target: 1,000 km EV range by 2027
learn.stat3: India: 50 GWh battery manufacturing target by 2030
learn.readLabel: Read at US Dept of Energy

learn.title: Building Integrated Photovoltaics: Solar as Architecture
learn.intro: Building Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV) turn roofs, windows, and facades into power generators. Instead of adding panels to existing buildings, BIPV makes solar a structural part of construction — creating energy-generating buildings from the ground up.
learn.c1_text: BIPV replaces conventional materials like glass or cladding with solar cells. Transparent photovoltaic windows let 40% of light through while generating power. Building facades can produce 20-30% of a structure's electricity needs without extra roof space.
learn.c2_text: Thin-film and perovskite solar cells are integrated into building materials. BIPV modules are rated for structural load, weather resistance, and fire safety. They operate at lower efficiency (10-15%) than rooftop panels but offset building materials costs and reduce cooling loads.
learn.c3_text: India's National Building Code promotes BIPV for new government buildings. The Solar Energy Corporation of India (SECI) has tendered 500 MW of BIPV projects. With 100 billion sq m new building stock expected by 2030, BIPV could add 50 GW of urban solar capacity.
learn.stat2: BIPV windows: 40% visible light transmission + power generation
learn.stat3: India's building stock growth: 100 billion sq m by 2030
learn.stat4: Solar share in new buildings: up to 30% of electricity needs
learn.readLabel: Read at US Dept of Energy

learn.title: Second Life EV Batteries: Grid Storage Reuse
learn.intro: EV batteries retain 70-80% capacity after 8-10 years in cars — still useful for grid storage. Second-life applications give these packs a second purpose before recycling, reducing waste and making renewable energy storage more affordable.
learn.c1_text: Like a retired runner becoming a coach, EV batteries at 70% capacity still work well for stationary storage. Nissan Leaf batteries now power streetlights in Japan and grid buffers in California, extending battery life by 10+ years and delaying recycling costs.
learn.c2_text: Second-life batteries cost 30-50% less than new ones for grid storage. A 2015 Leaf battery module (24 kWh) can provide 4 hours of backup for a home. However, testing and repackaging adds cost, and warranty uncertainty remains a challenge for commercial adoption.
learn.c3_text: India's EV market is projected to retire 200 GWh of batteries by 2030. Companies like Tata Power and Exide are piloting second-life storage for telecom towers and rural microgrids. The Battery Swapping Policy encourages reuse, targeting 50 GWh of second-life capacity by 2030.
learn.stat1: EV batteries retain 70-80% capacity after 8-10 years
learn.stat2: India: 200 GWh battery retirement projected by 2030
learn.stat3: Second-life cost savings: 30-50% vs new batteries
learn.stat4: India's second-life storage target: 50 GWh by 2030

learn.title: India's Green Hydrogen Mission: Targets and Incentives
learn.intro: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to make the country a global hub for clean hydrogen. By 2030, it targets 5 million tonnes annual production using renewable-powered electrolysis, supported by $2.4 billion in incentives and a production-linked incentive scheme.
learn.c1_text: Green hydrogen is made by splitting water using renewable electricity — like using sunshine or wind to break H2O into hydrogen and oxygen. Unlike grey hydrogen from fossil fuels, it produces zero CO2 emissions, making it a clean fuel for industry and transport.
learn.c2_text: India targets 5 MT green hydrogen annually, requiring 125 GW of new renewable capacity for electrolysis. The Hydrogen Hub in Andhra Pradesh and the National Hydrogen Mission aim to reduce import dependence and create 600,000 jobs in manufacturing, storage, and distribution.
learn.c3_text: The Green Hydrogen Standard sets a maximum 1 kg CO2 equivalent per kg H2 threshold. The Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) offers ₹50/kg for green hydrogen and ₹30/kg for green ammonia for 3 years, making India's subsidies among the world's most competitive for clean hydrogen.
learn.stat1: India target: 5 MT green hydrogen by 2030
learn.stat3: Renewable capacity needed: 125 GW for 5 MT target
learn.stat4: Global green hydrogen investment: $140 billion by 2030
